Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",

      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The Rust workload runner under `/app` sometimes leaves its audit stalled or inconsistent with bounded queues, cancelled branches, and child work mixed. Fix it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/executor-audit.json` for the shipped cases from the current inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array for amber, cobalt, drake, ember, flint, and graphite. Each case reports name, ok, finished, pending, trace, ledger, and journal. Trace is the accepted work records in acceptance order, where lower accepted turn comes first, lanes are ordered by name within a turn, and ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order. Every record carries case, task, parent, depth, lane, and turn; turn is when that record is accepted. Ledger points to a JSONL file containing the same records in the same order. Journal points to a text file with whitespace-delimited rows in the same order: case task parent depth lane turn.

A clean audit has every case finished, has no pending records, has ok true, preserves parent/depth relationships for child work, keeps cancelled branches and their descendants out of the accepted trace, and leaves the JSON, ledger, and journal in agreement. Child rows keep parent and depth on accepted records for hierarchy only; they do not wait for the named parent to be accepted first. Each row becomes eligible on its own arrival turn, and CAP, lane order, and plan-file order decide acceptance from there. Removing a branch drops that name and every descendant linked through the child chain, not only rows with an identical label. A CAP row limits how many ready records each lane may accept in one turn; overflow remains live for later turns and is considered alongside work that becomes ready later. The single-case helper at `/app/scripts/run-one.sh` must obey the same contract for one shipped case as the matrix run does for all cases. Repeated logical child entries should appear once in the accepted trace, and two fresh matrix runs from unchanged inputs should parse to equivalent audit data.

# Local runtime architecture

This repository contains a small deterministic workload runner used for local audit exercises. Plan files under `data/cases` describe rows that are loaded into
a local state graph, grouped by lane, and written to generated report surfaces under `/app/output`.

The source tree is split into short module roots. Each root owns one part of the local runtime: parsing, state transfer, ordering, row filtering, and line-orien
ted output. The split mirrors the production service that inspired this exercise, where generated JSON and append-only side files were checked by separate monit
oring jobs.
/app/docs/OPERATIONS.md
# Operations notes

Use `/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` for a full local audit run and `/app/scripts/run-one.sh NAME` for a single shipped plan. Both commands write generated files be
low `/app/output` and are safe to repeat because the matrix command starts by clearing that directory.

The Rust binary does not need network access. It reads local plan files, produces deterministic report rows, and exits with a nonzero status only when local fil
e I/O fails.

After a local release build, the executable path is `/app/target/release/executor-ctl`. The shipped plans use the lane-a and lane-b labels.

+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+import argparse
+import json
+import os
+from dataclasses import dataclass
+from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Set, Tuple
+
+
+CASE_ORDER = ["amber", "cobalt", "drake", "ember", "flint", "graphite"]
+
+
+@dataclass(frozen=True)
+class Row:
+    kind: str              # ROOT | CHILD
+    task: str
+    parent: Optional[str]  # None for ROOT
+    lane: str
+    ready_turn: int
+    plan_index: int
+
+
+def parse_plan(path: str) -> Tuple[int, List[Row], Set[str]]:
+    cap: Optional[int] = None
+    rows: List[Row] = []
+    drops: Set[str] = set()
+
+    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
+        for idx, raw in enumerate(f):
+            line = raw.strip()
+            if not line or line.startswith("#"):
+                continue
+            parts = line.split()
+            if parts[0] == "CAP":
+                if len(parts) != 2:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad CAP line: {line}")
+                cap = int(parts[1])
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "DROP":
+                if len(parts) != 2:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad DROP line: {line}")
+                drops.add(parts[1])
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "ROOT":
+                if len(parts) != 4:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad ROOT line: {line}")
+                _, task, lane, turn_s = parts
+                rows.append(Row("ROOT", task, None, lane, int(turn_s), idx))
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "CHILD":
+                if len(parts) != 5:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad CHILD line: {line}")
+                _, parent, task, lane, turn_s = parts
+                rows.append(Row("CHILD", task, parent, lane, int(turn_s), idx))
+                continue
+            raise ValueError(f"unknown line: {line}")
+
+    if cap is None:
+        raise ValueError(f"missing CAP in {path}")
+    return cap, rows, drops
+
+
+def drop_closure(rows: List[Row], drops: Set[str]) -> Set[str]:
+    children: Dict[str, List[str]] = {}
+    for r in rows:
+        if r.parent is not None:
+            children.setdefault(r.parent, []).append(r.task)
+
+    removed: Set[str] = set()
+    stack = list(drops)
+    while stack:
+        t = stack.pop()
+        if t in removed:
+            continue
+        removed.add(t)
+        for c in children.get(t, []):
+            if c not in removed:
+                stack.append(c)
+    return removed
+
+
+def compute_depth(task: str, parent_of: Dict[str, Optional[str]]) -> int:
+    depth = 0
+    seen: Set[str] = set()
+    cur = parent_of.get(task)
+    while cur is not None:
+        if cur in seen:
+            raise ValueError(f"cycle in parents at {task}")
+        seen.add(cur)
+        depth += 1
+        cur = parent_of.get(cur)
+    return depth
+
+
+def simulate(case: str, cap: int, rows: List[Row], drops: Set[str], work_dir: str) -> Dict:
+    removed = drop_closure(rows, drops)
+
+    # De-dup by logical task name: keep earliest plan entry.
+    by_task: Dict[str, Row] = {}
+    for r in rows:
+        if r.task in removed:
+            continue
+        prev = by_task.get(r.task)
+        if prev is None or r.plan_index < prev.plan_index:
+            by_task[r.task] = r
+
+    kept: List[Row] = sorted(by_task.values(), key=lambda r: r.plan_index)
+
+    parent_of: Dict[str, Optional[str]] = {r.task: r.parent for r in kept}
+    lane_of: Dict[str, str] = {r.task: r.lane for r in kept}
+    ready_of: Dict[str, int] = {r.task: r.ready_turn for r in kept}
+    plan_index_of: Dict[str, int] = {r.task: r.plan_index for r in kept}
+
+    max_turn = max([r.ready_turn for r in kept], default=0)
+
+    accepted_turn: Dict[str, int] = {}
+    trace: List[Dict] = []
+
+    lanes = sorted({r.lane for r in kept})
+
+    for turn in range(0, max_turn + 1):
+        # Per turn, process lanes in name order.
+        for lane in lanes:
+            ready_tasks = [
+                t for t in by_task.keys()
+                if t not in accepted_turn
+                and lane_of[t] == lane
+                and ready_of[t] <= turn
+            ]
+            # Stable: ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order.
+            ready_tasks.sort(key=lambda t: plan_index_of[t])
+            for t in ready_tasks[:cap]:
+                accepted_turn[t] = turn
+                trace.append(
+                    {
+                        "case": case,
+                        "task": t,
+                        "parent": parent_of.get(t),
+                        "depth": compute_depth(t, parent_of),
+                        "lane": lane,
+                        "turn": turn,
+                    }
+                )
+
+    # Any kept but unaccepted remain pending.
+    pending_tasks = [t for t in by_task.keys() if t not in accepted_turn]
+    pending_tasks.sort(key=lambda t: (ready_of[t], lane_of[t], plan_index_of[t]))
+    pending = [
+        {
+            "case": case,
+            "task": t,
+            "parent": parent_of.get(t),
+            "depth": compute_depth(t, parent_of),
+            "lane": lane_of[t],
+            "turn": None,
+        }
+        for t in pending_tasks
+    ]
+
+    os.makedirs(work_dir, exist_ok=True)
+    ledger_path = os.path.join(work_dir, f"{case}.ledger.jsonl")
+    journal_path = os.path.join(work_dir, f"{case}.journal.txt")
+
+    with open(ledger_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as lf, open(journal_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as jf:
+        for rec in trace:
+            lf.write(json.dumps(rec, sort_keys=True) + "\n")
+            parent = rec["parent"] if rec["parent"] is not None else "-"
+            jf.write(f"{rec['case']} {rec['task']} {parent} {rec['depth']} {rec['lane']} {rec['turn']}\n")
+
+    finished = (len(pending) == 0)
+    ok = finished
+    return {
+        "name": case,
+        "ok": ok,
+        "finished": finished,
+        "pending": pending,
+        "trace": trace,
+        "ledger": ledger_path,
+        "journal": journal_path,
+    }
+
+
+def main() -> None:
+    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="executor-ctl")
+    ap.add_argument("--case", dest="case", default=None)
+    ap.add_argument("--out", dest="out", required=True)
+    ap.add_argument("--work", dest="work", required=True)
+    args = ap.parse_args()
+
+    cases = [args.case] if args.case else CASE_ORDER
+    out_dir = os.path.dirname(args.out)
+    if out_dir:
+        os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)
+    os.makedirs(args.work, exist_ok=True)
+
+    case_objs = []
+    for c in cases:
+        plan_path = f"/app/data/cases/{c}.plan"
+        cap, rows, drops = parse_plan(plan_path)
+        case_objs.append(simulate(c, cap, rows, drops, args.work))
+
+    audit = {"cases": case_objs}
+    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
+        json.dump(audit, f, indent=2, sort_keys=True)
+        f.write("\n")
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    main()
+

Task Description:
The Rust workload runner under `/app` sometimes leaves its audit stalled or inconsistent with bounded queues, cancelled branches, and child work mixed. Fix it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/executor-audit.json` for the shipped cases from the current inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array for amber, cobalt, drake, ember, flint, and graphite. Each case reports name, ok, finished, pending, trace, ledger, and journal. Trace is the accepted work records in acceptance order, where lower accepted turn comes first, lanes are ordered by name within a turn, and ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order. Every record carries case, task, parent, depth, lane, and turn; turn is when that record is accepted. Ledger points to a JSONL file containing the same records in the same order. Journal points to a text file with whitespace-delimited rows in the same order: case task parent depth lane turn.

A clean audit has every case finished, has no pending records, has ok true, preserves parent/depth relationships for child work, keeps cancelled branches and their descendants out of the accepted trace, and leaves the JSON, ledger, and journal in agreement. Child rows keep parent and depth on accepted records for hierarchy only; they do not wait for the named parent to be accepted first. Each row becomes eligible on its own arrival turn, and CAP, lane order, and plan-file order decide acceptance from there. Removing a branch drops that name and every descendant linked through the child chain, not only rows with an identical label. A CAP row limits how many ready records each lane may accept in one turn; overflow remains live for later turns and is considered alongside work that becomes ready later. The single-case helper at `/app/scripts/run-one.sh` must obey the same contract for one shipped case as the matrix run does for all cases. Repeated logical child entries should appear once in the accepted trace, and two fresh matrix runs from unchanged inputs should parse to equivalent audit data.

# Local runtime architecture

This repository contains a small deterministic workload runner used for local audit exercises. Plan files under `data/cases` describe rows that are loaded into
a local state graph, grouped by lane, and written to generated report surfaces under `/app/output`.

The source tree is split into short module roots. Each root owns one part of the local runtime: parsing, state transfer, ordering, row filtering, and line-orien
ted output. The split mirrors the production service that inspired this exercise, where generated JSON and append-only side files were checked by separate monit
oring jobs.
/app/docs/OPERATIONS.md
# Operations notes

Use `/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` for a full local audit run and `/app/scripts/run-one.sh NAME` for a single shipped plan. Both commands write generated files be
low `/app/output` and are safe to repeat because the matrix command starts by clearing that directory.

The Rust binary does not need network access. It reads local plan files, produces deterministic report rows, and exits with a nonzero status only when local fil
e I/O fails.

After a local release build, the executable path is `/app/target/release/executor-ctl`. The shipped plans use the lane-a and lane-b labels.

Task Description:
The Rust workload runner under `/app` sometimes leaves its audit stalled or inconsistent with bounded queues, cancelled branches, and child work mixed. Fix it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/executor-audit.json` for the shipped cases from the current inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array for amber, cobalt, drake, ember, flint, and graphite. Each case reports name, ok, finished, pending, trace, ledger, and journal. Trace is the accepted work records in acceptance order, where lower accepted turn comes first, lanes are ordered by name within a turn, and ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order. Every record carries case, task, parent, depth, lane, and turn; turn is when that record is accepted. Ledger points to a JSONL file containing the same records in the same order. Journal points to a text file with whitespace-delimited rows in the same order: case task parent depth lane turn.

A clean audit has every case finished, has no pending records, has ok true, preserves parent/depth relationships for child work, keeps cancelled branches and their descendants out of the accepted trace, and leaves the JSON, ledger, and journal in agreement. Child rows keep parent and depth on accepted records for hierarchy only; they do not wait for the named parent to be accepted first. Each row becomes eligible on its own arrival turn, and CAP, lane order, and plan-file order decide acceptance from there. Removing a branch drops that name and every descendant linked through the child chain, not only rows with an identical label. A CAP row limits how many ready records each lane may accept in one turn; overflow remains live for later turns and is considered alongside work that becomes ready later. The single-case helper at `/app/scripts/run-one.sh` must obey the same contract for one shipped case as the matrix run does for all cases. Repeated logical child entries should appear once in the accepted trace, and two fresh matrix runs from unchanged inputs should parse to equivalent audit data.

# Local runtime architecture

This repository contains a small deterministic workload runner used for local audit exercises. Plan files under `data/cases` describe rows that are loaded into
a local state graph, grouped by lane, and written to generated report surfaces under `/app/output`.

The source tree is split into short module roots. Each root owns one part of the local runtime: parsing, state transfer, ordering, row filtering, and line-orien
ted output. The split mirrors the production service that inspired this exercise, where generated JSON and append-only side files were checked by separate monit
oring jobs.
/app/docs/OPERATIONS.md
# Operations notes

Use `/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` for a full local audit run and `/app/scripts/run-one.sh NAME` for a single shipped plan. Both commands write generated files be
low `/app/output` and are safe to repeat because the matrix command starts by clearing that directory.

The Rust binary does not need network access. It reads local plan files, produces deterministic report rows, and exits with a nonzero status only when local fil
e I/O fails.

After a local release build, the executable path is `/app/target/release/executor-ctl`. The shipped plans use the lane-a and lane-b labels.

+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+import argparse
+import json
+import os
+from dataclasses import dataclass
+from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Set, Tuple
+
+
+CASE_ORDER = ["amber", "cobalt", "drake", "ember", "flint", "graphite"]
+
+
+@dataclass(frozen=True)
+class Row:
+    kind: str              # ROOT | CHILD
+    task: str
+    parent: Optional[str]  # None for ROOT
+    lane: str
+    ready_turn: int
+    plan_index: int
+
+
+def parse_plan(path: str) -> Tuple[int, List[Row], Set[str]]:
+    cap: Optional[int] = None
+    rows: List[Row] = []
+    drops: Set[str] = set()
+
+    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
+        for idx, raw in enumerate(f):
+            line = raw.strip()
+            if not line or line.startswith("#"):
+                continue
+            parts = line.split()
+            if parts[0] == "CAP":
+                if len(parts) != 2:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad CAP line: {line}")
+                cap = int(parts[1])
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "DROP":
+                if len(parts) != 2:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad DROP line: {line}")
+                drops.add(parts[1])
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "ROOT":
+                if len(parts) != 4:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad ROOT line: {line}")
+                _, task, lane, turn_s = parts
+                rows.append(Row("ROOT", task, None, lane, int(turn_s), idx))
+                continue
+            if parts[0] == "CHILD":
+                if len(parts) != 5:
+                    raise ValueError(f"bad CHILD line: {line}")
+                _, parent, task, lane, turn_s = parts
+                rows.append(Row("CHILD", task, parent, lane, int(turn_s), idx))
+                continue
+            raise ValueError(f"unknown line: {line}")
+
+    if cap is None:
+        raise ValueError(f"missing CAP in {path}")
+    return cap, rows, drops
+
+
+def drop_closure(rows: List[Row], drops: Set[str]) -> Set[str]:
+    children: Dict[str, List[str]] = {}
+    for r in rows:
+        if r.parent is not None:
+            children.setdefault(r.parent, []).append(r.task)
+
+    removed: Set[str] = set()
+    stack = list(drops)
+    while stack:
+        t = stack.pop()
+        if t in removed:
+            continue
+        removed.add(t)
+        for c in children.get(t, []):
+            if c not in removed:
+                stack.append(c)
+    return removed
+
+
+def compute_depth(task: str, parent_of: Dict[str, Optional[str]]) -> int:
+    depth = 0
+    seen: Set[str] = set()
+    cur = parent_of.get(task)
+    while cur is not None:
+        if cur in seen:
+            raise ValueError(f"cycle in parents at {task}")
+        seen.add(cur)
+        depth += 1
+        cur = parent_of.get(cur)
+    return depth
+
+
+def simulate(case: str, cap: int, rows: List[Row], drops: Set[str], work_dir: str) -> Dict:
+    removed = drop_closure(rows, drops)
+
+    # De-dup by logical task name: keep earliest plan entry.
+    by_task: Dict[str, Row] = {}
+    for r in rows:
+        if r.task in removed:
+            continue
+        prev = by_task.get(r.task)
+        if prev is None or r.plan_index < prev.plan_index:
+            by_task[r.task] = r
+
+    kept: List[Row] = sorted(by_task.values(), key=lambda r: r.plan_index)
+
+    parent_of: Dict[str, Optional[str]] = {r.task: r.parent for r in kept}
+    lane_of: Dict[str, str] = {r.task: r.lane for r in kept}
+    ready_of: Dict[str, int] = {r.task: r.ready_turn for r in kept}
+    plan_index_of: Dict[str, int] = {r.task: r.plan_index for r in kept}
+
+    max_turn = max([r.ready_turn for r in kept], default=0)
+
+    accepted_turn: Dict[str, int] = {}
+    trace: List[Dict] = []
+
+    lanes = sorted({r.lane for r in kept})
+
+    for turn in range(0, max_turn + 1):
+        # Per turn, process lanes in name order.
+        for lane in lanes:
+            ready_tasks = [
+                t for t in by_task.keys()
+                if t not in accepted_turn
+                and lane_of[t] == lane
+                and ready_of[t] <= turn
+            ]
+            # Stable: ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order.
+            ready_tasks.sort(key=lambda t: plan_index_of[t])
+            for t in ready_tasks[:cap]:
+                accepted_turn[t] = turn
+                trace.append(
+                    {
+                        "case": case,
+                        "task": t,
+                        "parent": parent_of.get(t),
+                        "depth": compute_depth(t, parent_of),
+                        "lane": lane,
+                        "turn": turn,
+                    }
+                )
+
+    # Any kept but unaccepted remain pending.
+    pending_tasks = [t for t in by_task.keys() if t not in accepted_turn]
+    pending_tasks.sort(key=lambda t: (ready_of[t], lane_of[t], plan_index_of[t]))
+    pending = [
+        {
+            "case": case,
+            "task": t,
+            "parent": parent_of.get(t),
+            "depth": compute_depth(t, parent_of),
+            "lane": lane_of[t],
+            "turn": None,
+        }
+        for t in pending_tasks
+    ]
+
+    os.makedirs(work_dir, exist_ok=True)
+    ledger_path = os.path.join(work_dir, f"{case}.ledger.jsonl")
+    journal_path = os.path.join(work_dir, f"{case}.journal.txt")
+
+    with open(ledger_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as lf, open(journal_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as jf:
+        for rec in trace:
+            lf.write(json.dumps(rec, sort_keys=True) + "\n")
+            parent = rec["parent"] if rec["parent"] is not None else "-"
+            jf.write(f"{rec['case']} {rec['task']} {parent} {rec['depth']} {rec['lane']} {rec['turn']}\n")
+
+    finished = (len(pending) == 0)
+    ok = finished
+    return {
+        "name": case,
+        "ok": ok,
+        "finished": finished,
+        "pending": pending,
+        "trace": trace,
+        "ledger": ledger_path,
+        "journal": journal_path,
+    }
+
+
+def main() -> None:
+    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="executor-ctl")
+    ap.add_argument("--case", dest="case", default=None)
+    ap.add_argument("--out", dest="out", required=True)
+    ap.add_argument("--work", dest="work", required=True)
+    args = ap.parse_args()
+
+    cases = [args.case] if args.case else CASE_ORDER
+    out_dir = os.path.dirname(args.out)
+    if out_dir:
+        os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)
+    os.makedirs(args.work, exist_ok=True)
+
+    case_objs = []
+    for c in cases:
+        plan_path = f"/app/data/cases/{c}.plan"
+        cap, rows, drops = parse_plan(plan_path)
+        case_objs.append(simulate(c, cap, rows, drops, args.work))
+
+    audit = {"cases": case_objs}
+    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
+        json.dump(audit, f, indent=2, sort_keys=True)
+        f.write("\n")
+
+
+if __name__ == "__main__":
+    main()
+
